paper_title: Peatland Development, Vegetation History, Climate Change and Human Activity in the Valdai Uplands (Central European Russia) during the Holocene: A Multi-Proxy Palaeoecological Study

methods.description: Process for preparing and analyzing macro charcoal samples to determine fire episodes and frequency using CharAnalysis software.

steps.description: Samples (volume of 1 cm3) were bleached with a water solution of NaOCl and were washed with distilled water using a sieve with a mesh size of 125 µm.
steps.searchable_summary: bleach and wash samples using NaOCl and sieve

steps.description: All materials in the sieve were carefully transferred to labeled Petri dishes, and all charcoal particles were tallied under a dissecting light microscope at the magnification of 40×.
steps.searchable_summary: transfer and count charcoal particles under microscope

steps.description: Data processing of macro charcoal concentration was carried out in a modified version of the CharAnalysis software developed by Higuera et al.
steps.searchable_summary: process charcoal data using CharAnalysis software

steps.description: To determine fire episodes and fire frequency, the following parameters were used: CHAR-charcoal accumulation rate (pieces cm−2 yr−1), Cint-CHAR of interpolated record, Cback-low-frequency trend in Cint, Cpeak-high-frequency trends in Cint.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate fire episodes and frequency using charcoal parameters

steps.description: Fire episodes were defined as a ratio (Cpeak = Cint / Cback). These peaks were separated from background charcoal (Cback) using the smoothing method LOWESS.
steps.searchable_summary: define fire episodes using LOWESS smoothing

steps.description: A 1000-year window was applied to smoothen the record over for estimating Cback.
The associated threshold value within the Cpeak component was set at the 90th percentile of a Gaussian mixture model.
steps.searchable_summary: set Cpeak threshold using Gaussian mixture model

methods.description: Procedure for preparing and analyzing pollen samples to reconstruct mean July temperature using a pollen-climate calibration set.

steps.description: Samples for pollen analysis (volume~1 cm3) were treated using the acetolysis method.
steps.searchable_summary: treat pollen samples using acetolysis method

steps.description: The sample preparation included wet sieving through a 500 µm mesh, treatment with 10% KOH in a water bath for 5 min followed by repeated washing with distilled water, dehydration with ice-cold acetic acid and acetolysis followed by washing.
steps.searchable_summary: prepare samples with sieving, KOH treatment, and acetolysis

steps.description: Counting and identification of spores and pollen were carried out in temporary glycerin slides with a light microscope at 400× magnification according to the standard method.
steps.searchable_summary: count and identify pollen using light microscope

steps.description: The relative abundance of pollen was calculated to the total pollen sum of arboreal pollen (AP) and non-arboreal pollen (NAP), excluding pollen and spores of aquatic plants.

The pollen data were used to reconstruct the mean July temperature based on the pollen-climate calibration set derived from the European Modern Pollen Dataset using the transfer functions developed by Salonen et al.
steps.searchable_summary: reconstruct mean July temperature using pollen data

steps.description: A weighted averaging-partial least-squares model (WAPLS: 3-components, RMSEP = 1.73, Maximum bias = 5.90) was applied to square-root transformed species data.
steps.searchable_summary: apply WAPLS model to transformed species data

steps.description: The analyses and visualization were implemented with the R packages 'rioja' and 'analogue'.
steps.searchable_summary: visualize analysis results using R packages
